title: Vegetable Pakoda (Vegetable Fritters)

ingredients.title: Ingredients
items: Spinach leaves – 1 cup (loosely packed, about 50 g)
Green chili – 1–2, finely chopped (adjust to taste)
Carom seeds (Ajwain) – ½ tsp (aids digestion, adds aroma)
Baking soda – a pinch (optional, for extra lightness)

ingredients.title: For Frying
items: Neutral-flavored oil (sunflower/vegetable oil) – enough for deep frying (about 500 ml in a wok/kadhai)

instruction.title: Prep Vegetables
steps: Wash and peel vegetables as needed.
Slice onions thinly, julienne potato and carrot, roughly chop spinach, and finely chop green chilies.
Pat vegetables dry to avoid excess water in the batter.

instruction.title: Make the Batter
steps: In a large mixing bowl, add gram flour, rice flour, chili powder, turmeric, coriander powder, ajwain, baking soda, and salt.
Gradually add water while mixing until you get a thick, lump-free batter that coats vegetables but is not runny.

instruction.title: Combine Vegetables & Batter
steps: Add the sliced vegetables to the batter.
Toss and mix so all vegetables are well coated.
Rest for 5–7 minutes to allow salt to draw moisture from onions and enhance flavor.

instruction.title: Heat the Oil
steps: In a deep wok/kadhai, heat oil over medium heat to 170–175°C (340–350°F).
Test by dropping a small bit of batter into oil — it should sizzle and rise slowly to the top.

instruction.title: Fry the Pakodas
steps: Drop small portions of the batter-coated vegetables into the hot oil, using your fingers or a spoon.
Avoid overcrowding to maintain oil temperature.
Fry, turning occasionally, until golden brown and crisp — about 3–4 minutes per batch.
Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels.

instruction.title: Serve
steps: Serve hot with green chutney, tamarind chutney, or tomato ketchup.
Sprinkle lightly with chaat masala for a professional street-food touch.

chefTips: Rice flour is key for crispness — do not skip it.
The batter should be just thick enough to bind vegetables; excess batter makes them heavy.
Maintain consistent oil temperature — too low will make pakodas oily, too high will brown them before cooking inside.
For extra flavor, add finely chopped coriander leaves and a pinch of garam masala to the batter.
These are best enjoyed fresh — they lose crispness if stored.
